data.title: SPYI Vs. QDPL: The More Reliable Income Strategy Across Cycles
data.description:
On paper, the methodology embedded in the Pacer Metaurus US Large Cap Dividend Multiplier 400 ETF (QDPL) looks very promising compared to covered call-based strategies. This is because it appears to allow undeterred upside capture in S&P 500 rallies that an option strategy restricts theoretically. QDPL is indeed a strong income play, but its thesis actually works out in narrow conditions - conditions we have seen in the few years since its inception. But QDPL does not retain its edge as an income plan over covered call plays when it comes to recessions, sharp drawdowns, or even consolidating markets. That makes it less reliable in market conditions I am bracing for in 2026, which indicates anything but a sharp rally.

This thesis dives into the mechanics of QDPL and compares its relative utility versus a popular covered call alternative, the NEOS S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI). It finds QDPL a decent portfolio Hold, for market conditions ahead, but I would continue to rate SPYI as a Buy and position it as overweight within the income portfolio. SPYI has survived a comparison with a methodologically superior covered call play, GPIX, the last time I reviewed it. And it again survives a comparison with another apparently stronger play like QDPL. SPYI could actually be the ETF to own in markets that are unlikely to need upside capture like it has in the past few years of bull market conditions. Even when it comes to that, the perceived gap does not really show up as much as implied.

Why I Like SPYI

First of all, let us understand why I present SPYI as the representative covered call play in this comparison. This is because SPYI has done really well despite more promising challengers, such as GPIX. The GPIX versus SPYI was the backbone of my most recent SPYI Buy call, so I will start from there and see whether SPYI continues to command the Buy rating within its peer space, especially since the markets have stalled and even corrected a bit since then.

In that last thesis written in October last year, I had shown how SPYI has no business matching a more flexible strategy like GPIX on paper, yet it often holds up far better than expected. The gap narrows because what looks like a structural limitation in SPYI actually functions as a stabilizing feature across most market environments. GPIX’s variable coverage framework offers clear theoretical advantages, but those benefits depend on how effectively they are implemented. When positioning is not aligned with market moves, the intended edge can diminish quickly, as I have observed recently.

SPYI, by contrast, derives strength from a more consistent structure. Its call spread approach retains a portion of upside while still generating income, which reduces the need for precise adjustments. Additionally, SPYI's far higher allowed portfolio coverage (not capped explicitly at 75% like GPIX) becomes particularly relevant in range-bound markets, where more premium can be harvested.

Since October, in relatively less bullish conditions, I do not see much gap in total returns between SPYI and GPIX. In short, SPYI continues to defend its more popular footing as an all-weather, dependable covered call ETF (AUM over $8 billion versus GPIX's ~$3.3 billion)—simpler structure and more reliable, especially in range-bound markets. And, as the charts below show, it does trace the underlying index quite well for rebounds/rallies of 5-6% at a stretch (like in November 2025), so the upside capping fears kick in only when we are talking about significantly sharp rallies. There too, the opportunity lost should not be huge, as SPYI does implement a covered call spread structure, reopening rally capture after a higher strike.

What Does QDPL Do

That's where we park SPYI, a reliable income plan that does not really lag the S&P 500 unless we are talking about a sharp rally like say 10% within a short period of time (~4% rebound within a month has been captured well in November, as we can see, and slower rebounds until January 2026 have seen SPYI do better than even SPY in total return terms). It does punch above its weight in real-world performance. And that is why we bring SPYI as a covered call alternative to QDPL's dividend amplification strategy.

Now let's turn to the challenger, QDPL (AUM ~$1.5 billion), and understand what it does. QDPL is best viewed as an S&P exposure plus a leveraged bet on dividend expectations, not a simple high dividend strategy. The strategy allocates ~90% to the S&P 500 and adds a dividend component targeting ~400% of the index’s normal dividend exposure using exchange-traded dividend futures.

The S&P 500 exposure is implemented as physical stock holdings, and that accounts for an assured layer of dividends from it. That exposure can vary around the 90% mark depending on how much cash is required to implement the leveraged dividend futures position (or market conditions). The remaining capital is parked in Treasuries or income assets to act as collateral for the leveraged futures position (they add modest yield, often offsetting leverage and derivative costs).

What QDPL Needs to Work

In the case of QDPPL, the amplified dividend leg earns the difference between actual and expected dividends, not the full dividend itself. That means it needs a very narrow market condition to do better than the S&P 500—a condition where actual dividends beat expectations meaningfully. QDPL's performance has nothing to do with the absolute dividend regime. It could see drags even in high dividend regimes if that's already embedded in market expectations.

Even in favorable scenarios where actual dividends exceed expectations, the differential must be sufficiently wide. The leveraged dividend overlay needs to generate enough excess return to offset the drag from lower S&P 500 exposure before contributing positively to relative performance.

Take, for example, a case where the S&P 500 has rallied 5% over a period. Since QDPL has only ~90% exposure to this, it captures ~4% upside from the underlying portfolio. During this same period, it needs to not only see its dividend futures bet succeed (i.e., actual dividend turning out to be more than expected), but the dividend outperformance should be meaningful enough, such that amplification more than offsets its lower portfolio return, say effectively more than a 0.25% difference between the actual and expected dividends, so that a 4x amplification overrides the lower S&P 500 exposure. This is, of course, a simplistic illustrative example that does not account for the dividends from the stocks held or treasury yields and leverage costs. But it helps explain how QDPL needs to get the bet right and also by a certain magnitude to be able to outperform the S&P 500.

SPYI, on the other hand, monetizes volatility and time rather than forecasting accuracy. It does not require being right about future outcomes; it only needs markets that are not persistently trending sharply upward (sharper than ~4% a month, say). In other words, QDPL requires being directionally and quantitatively right on dividends, whereas SPYI appears to be a broader income strategy that performs across a wider range of outcomes.

Fixing the Market Variable

The wider applicability of SPYI and its higher odds of doing well versus QDPL across most market conditions (as discussed in the previous section) is not a mutually exclusive case, though. We could be in markets where both do well or both underperform — the performance criteria for each are independent of the other. And there are other nuances too, such as income and NAV repercussions. So, fixing the market conditions helps focus the thesis.

Now let's focus on the forward view. What we are already seeing is a slower and choppy market where SPYI should add definite value because it will earn income from premiums both if markets consolidate or correct further. It will keep up with slower upsides, and SPYI's recent performance shows us glimpses of this expectation already playing out.

The next few quarters are likely to see slower headline growth in the S&P 500. There is a mix of geopolitical uncertainty, rates staying higher for longer, and AI trades cooling after valuation extensions in 2024-25. The CapEx drag in hyperscalers is also capping upsides (and S&P 500 is dominated by growth tech with long-duration assumptions).

The tech promise and secular AI tailwinds are not broken, though. I fully expect markets to hold up and not see further severe drawdowns from here. The balanced view is net consolidation with volatility over the next few quarters, until the economics of the AI ecosystem (and how CapEx returns shape up) and AI impact in areas like software (under selling pressure today due to uncertainties) become clearer. A consolidating market is SPYI territory. SPYI's methodology of covered call spreads could add premium income here, and the NAV should also remain resilient.

It is important to understand that SPYI can track the S&P 500 relatively well if there is a slower rebound of up to 4-5% too (as we have seen in November 2025). In drawdowns, SPYI will add premium income and mitigate generally 2-3 percent of an S&P 500 fall. However, NAV erosion comes into play because of the dual impact of the underlying portfolio correcting and payouts exceeding the option income in this case.

As we have discussed before, a favorable market condition for SPYI (i.e., flat markets with volatility) does not necessarily mean underperformance in QDPL because it needs actual dividend outcomes to be higher than expectations, something that could happen irrespective of market conditions. However, the 2026 outlook is not only that of a headline index consolidation/pressure due to valuations, but one where earnings are slower than earlier. I have presented a detailed analysis showing slowing earnings upgrade momentum in the top S&P 500 components in another article. That may be more applicable for tech and less so for financials and utilities, but it is also true that non-tech sectors generally operate with more stable and predictable payout policies. Dividends are closely tied to earnings visibility, capital requirements, and regulatory frameworks. That leaves little room for positive surprises structurally, especially when earnings become slower due to higher than expected funding and operating costs in a sticky inflation and higher rates environment.

QDPL also needs dividend surprises, not stability. To that end, the SPY dividend growth has been slowing since the rebound from the 2020 and 2021-22 markets. That means, even if there are surprises, amplifying a smaller difference at best is unlikely to match the regime QDPL has been through in the recent past.

Long-Term Thesis - Beyond Current Markets

For the long-term thesis, one of the misleading graphs that often point favorably for QDPL is the total return charts since 2022-23, where we see QDPL do far better than SPYI in what has been a unidirectionally bullish market for the S&P 500. That often supports the thesis that even SPYI's theoretical option strategy implies — the thesis that SPYI is not for strongly bullish markets.

That is not entirely true. Historical charts can narrate different stories depending on the time period used. In this case, if you take out SPYI's lag as markets took off in 2024, you will see SPYI has not really suffered from its lack of ability to capture upside. The total return chart below is plotted from mid-2024 until date and shows how SPYI has been ahead of QDPL and almost tracked the S&P 500 in a strong bull market, especially the strong run from the April 2025 lows. If SPYI can debunk its bull market underperformance expectations in these strong markets, we are essentially talking about rare strong rebounds only (even sharper than April 2025 onwards) when SPYI could suffer. That's a lot rarer than what QDPL needs to underperform. The starting problem in 2023-24 is a one-off for SPYI, something not seen in more recent history.

Coming to QDPL, its outperformance versus the S&P 500 needs very rare market conditions to emerge. SPYI at least can and has outperformed in the slower markets since October last year. QDPL needs to get dividend expectations right and by a strong magnitude to be able to outperform the S&P 500. In fact, if the actual dividends are lower than expected, QDPL could be strongly penalized. That's a relevant and material problem in pressured markets (like, say, a recession) we have not seen since its inception.

While not a precise indicator of dividend expectations versus actual outcomes, a regime of strongly accelerating dividend growth can serve as a broad proxy for when QDPL is more likely to work. Periods where dividend growth meaningfully picks up tend to coincide with positive surprises relative to expectations, which is where the strategy’s leveraged positioning can add value. However, as the historical data suggests, such accelerations are not necessarily seen across regimes (refer to the dividend and growth chart for SPY in the previous section). In most environments, dividend growth remains steady and predictable, limiting the scope for meaningful outperformance (or limited leverage benefits if the difference is low).

QDPL is likely to struggle in periods where dividends either decline (or fall short of expectations), which could be rare, but markets where SPYI could continue to do well, at least in total return terms. Regimes like the 2008-09 financial crisis or even the softer phases around 2015-16 saw dividend growth stall or turn negative, which could have translated into unfavorable outcomes for QDPL's strategy dependent on positive surprises. These are not edge cases but part of normal market cycles. Such conditions have largely been absent in the post-2021 period since QDPL's inception, which coincides with its decent performance.

Keep Both, But Overweight SPYI

QDPL does have some advantages that should be highlighted (justifying the "strong" Hold rating). Firstly, more than 80% of the income in 2025 was classified as return of capital (ROC). That means deferred taxation and potential classification as capital gains eventually - more efficient than a primarily dividend income payout classified as ordinary income and taxed at income tax slabs. Overall, QDPL is reasonably tax-friendly. But so is SPYI with significant ROC. In March 2026, SPYI's payout had around 97% ROC. These distribution characteristics could vary across regimes for both the ETFs, but overall QDPL is not necessarily a more tax-efficient ETF.

The only real relative advantage in QDPL is its lower yield levels that preserve greater NAV, while SPYI's yields are higher and could be NAV erosive over time. Reinvestments are not efficient because of tax events introduced. But it is equally true that QDPL could also return capital if its income generation engine falters. Or it could see visibly lower yields, like it did in early 2025.

Overall, balancing the long-term thesis, short-term market outlook, and structural pros and cons, I feel that for the regime ahead, I would overweight SPYI in my income portfolio. The expected performance gap trumps tax and NAV erosion concerns. Hence, SPYI retains a Buy rating. QDPL is not necessarily going to perform worse, but it is not as versatile. I rate it as a strong Hold for an income portfolio riding the S&P 500.

data.title: Private Credit: Why It's Not 2008
data.description:
If you open the financial newspapers today, you get a sense of déjà vu. The thousands of stories about troubled funds offered by newspapers and news sites, blocked redemption requests, and uncollectable loans seem to have come straight from the headlines that preceded the subprime mortgage disaster. Everyone's screaming about the collapse of the system. I must say that even I was very superficially convinced of this. I, too, began to think that everything would soon collapse. Then I remembered that my job is precisely to understand what's behind things, analyze, and get to the bottom of the news, and I have all the tools to do so. I work in the sector professionally and have access to a lot of software and subscriptions that are expensive but offer so much added value.

So one day I stopped and said, "Are we really in a new 2008 like everyone is telling us?" Pause for a moment because betting on a sudden Lehman Brothers-style financial "heart attack" is the worst tactical mistake you can make today.

Before writing this article, I wasn't sure how to approach it. Many analysts and newspapers are talking about it, and I wanted to add value. I wanted to offer my perspective as well as report the facts objectively. So it took me time not only to gather and select the right information but also, and above all, to figure out how to interpret this analysis. Then I thought: if I were a reader of Seeking Alpha, what would I really want to read today? My answer was "an instruction manual." And here it is, your instruction manual to understand what's really happening and, above all, how to position your portfolio to avoid being crushed (and perhaps emerge a winner).

Heart Attack vs. Chronic Illness

The reason I'm bringing up a heart attack is that the markets literally suffered one. It was in 2008. Banks had toxic derivatives that were all tied to residential retail mortgages. These derivatives went bust. In the movie "Margin Call," you see the events of the last night. That is why we are discussing a heart attack.

We are not dealing with the same type of heart attack today. Instead, we are dealing with a chronic, long-term, painful disease. Unlike banks, private credit funds have the ability to contractually restrict investors from withdrawing their money using what are called "gates" (usually 5% per quarter repayment limitations). This is not the type of event that causes an immediate collapse on Wall Street. Instead, this is the type of event where thousands of small to medium-sized businesses will be zombified due to high interest rates and will not be able to grow.

Do you think you're out of it because you're not a millionaire? Wrong. Thanks to a regulatory change last year, private credit funds were included within pension plans (like 401ks), so (I'm sorry to tell you if you didn't know) unknowingly, the life savings of millions of people are sitting on what analysts call a gigantic "game of musical chairs." And when the music stops, there won't be enough seats for everyone. But don't be too scared; finish reading this article, and you'll understand better.

A Timeline of Disaster: How It All Went Down

Note to readers: At this point, I can assume that investors that follow the private credit market closely on a daily basis are well aware of what contributed to the current state of the market.

Below, I'll provide the chronology of events (I'll go back a bit in time), so if you all know how we got here, feel free to skip this section. Otherwise, make yourself comfortable; it might be an enjoyable (or perhaps not) re-reading of what happened.

I tried to highlight every aspect that relates to the problem of private credit, but if I've missed any steps, please let me know in the comments, adding more value to this analysis. Now, we can continue moving forward from here.

First, and as I said before, I'd like to point out that I'm going back a little further in time. I believe the real problems began a few years ago and that the effects are only now being seen. Let's start at the source.

2020 - 2023: The Golden Age

During the period of zero interest rates, private credit explodes. Middle-market firms (that were largely funded by private equity firms) were being given all sorts of free-flowing money. The loans had variable interest rates. At the time, this appeared to be an attractive option for investors because when central banks started raising interest rates violently in 2022–2023, the interest payments for these companies skyrocketed. However, formal default rates remained very low. So why didn't companies just default? The answer is simple. Private credit fund managers increasingly relied on mechanisms such as:

  • "Amend and Extend": A loan cannot be repaid as scheduled by a company. The fund manager will extend the time for the company to make repayment. As well, the fund manager may alter the original terms of the loan agreement; typically with an increased rate of interest. This way, the problem is put off until a better time.
  • "Payment-in-Kind" (PIK): When a company has insufficient funds to make the required payments to the lender, PIK is used. PIK means instead of requiring the lender to receive the required interest in cash, the lender receives the interest as additional principal to the original loan.

As a result, the issue was simply ignored.

2024: Problems in the fundamentals

With higher interest rates for longer, the debt burden became unaffordable for the real economy. In mid-2024, Standard & Poor's (S&P) and Fitch announced that they were observing many more private company ratings "downgrades" than ratings "updates." This was the first time the true liquidity mismatch of this market was recognized. Funds stated that they could allow investors to withdraw their money every quarter. However, capital was invested in illiquid loans with maturities between five and seven years.

2025: The First Crash and Repayment Stops

In 2025, things start to go wrong, and the financial illusion starts to crumble. Early in 2025, the International Monetary Fund (IMF) releases an alarming report that states that approximately 40% of companies that were financed by private credit are generating negative cash flow. This means those companies do not generate sufficient revenue to cover the costs of interest payments. In September, the first large-scale bankruptcies begin to occur (such as the bankruptcies of First Brands Group and Tricolor), and liquidity panic sets in. Because of increasing redemption requests, several leading firms in the industry restrict access to their funds (industry-wide "gating"), such as Cliffwater, which can only fulfill approximately 50% of redemption requests, and Morgan Stanley, which also imposes similar restrictions.

The liquidity crisis, or the run on "shadow credit" banks, is indeed happening. It is evident from the chart in the Financial Times that the number of redemption requests from investors seeking to withdraw from private credit funds (including Blue Owl and Morgan Stanley) has exceeded the warning threshold, and therefore, the fund managers have restricted all withdrawals.

Early 2026 (Today): The "SaaSpocalypse"

We are currently in a time when there is an extreme disruption of the markets caused by the combination of two very negative forces:

  • The AI Shock: Many private equity firms have made significant investments into B2B software companies, which had previously been viewed as being more stable than most other investment options because they generated revenue from subscription-based services. This all changed quickly with the emergence of the rapidly developing artificial intelligence industry. As such, many of the B2B software companies that private equity firms had invested in became obsolete overnight. Experts are calling the events taking place in the software sector today "The SaaSpocalypse" as they see loan values falling off a cliff in the software sector.

As you can see from this chart, most of the investment exposure in private credit portfolios was in B2B and IT software. Unfortunately, this was a huge mistake, given that the advent of AI caused a complete change in the way the software sector operates.

  • Rising Stress and Higher Default Activity: Blackstone’s enormous private credit fund (BCRED, $83 billion) recorded its first monthly loss in over three years in February. As a result of large-scale redemption requests, Blackstone used the personal funds of its top-level management team to repay fleeing investors. Furthermore, according to Fitch Ratings, the default rate in private credit has risen to 9.2%.

Therefore, I firmly believe that the primary concern today is that the collapse could lead to a "contagion". Traditional banks have extended over $250 billion in loans to these private credit funds. Therefore, if the funds collapse under the burden of defaults and redemption requests, the losses would be passed through directly to the bank balance sheet.

Credit Structuring And Rising Stress Signals

The major investment banks are not at all surprised by the present crisis. While they are giving you the impression that there is nothing to worry about, in reality they are working hard to create the instruments for their top hedge fund clients to short (bet against) the private credit market, which totals $1.8 trillion. There are some structural similarities with past credit cycles, particularly in the use of securitization and risk transfer mechanisms. It gets worse.

In today's market, the behemoths of the industry are finding themselves with hundreds of millions of dollars in their mutual fund portfolios in the form of "trouble loans" (the ones issued to the failed software companies mentioned above). Therefore, how do they protect their interests?

Possible methods include the use of CLOs (Collateralized Loan Obligations), where high-risk (or "stressed") loans are bundled into one product and sold on the market, spreading the credit risk among the various tranches. Investors may purchase the same portfolio of underlying assets with varying levels of credit risk. Theoretically, this aggregation of similar assets will improve both the comprehensive diversification and liquidity of the system. Nevertheless, it is not uncommon for this type of process to create "a veil" for the true credit quality of a loan, particularly at the end of a business cycle and/or with regard to more complicated financial products.

Musical Chairs & Natural Selection: my predictions

What will happen next? Aaron Brown, a Bloomberg analyst, said something perfectly fitting when he referred to the sector as now having simply started a ferocious game of musical chairs.

Debtors do not possess the funds needed to make payments. Institutional funds do not possess sufficient liquidity to repay their investors. And everybody is frantically attempting to collect each and every last cent available to them before the music stops.

Over the next 12-24 months we shall clearly observe 3 distinct characteristics.

#1 A panic develops in the secondary market.

Secondary market transactions have reached an astronomical level of $225B in 2025 (source: Bloomberg Terminal). This suggests a significant increase in liquidity-driven secondary market activity. Take a look at how the secondary market transaction volume exploded upward in 2025.

#2 The sharks will be waiting for the blood to run.

If you were thinking that the large institutional investors in distressed debt will immediately step forward to purchase the distressed loans to rescue the distressed loan market by purchasing these distressed loans, you are mistaken. They will not buy these distressed loan portfolios because many of the distressed loans being sold today are of very low quality. The real "sharks" of finance don't buy bad loans at the bottom of the first downturn but patiently wait for the funds to fail and then acquire those assets at a fraction of their original value. Another déjà vu?

#3 The stealthy erosion of retail portfolios

We will not hear about the implosion of the distressed loan market in the evening news because the "gates" (the withdrawal restrictions) will allow the hemorrhaging to slow. What we will instead witness is the silent eroding of retail portfolio values. Dividend payments from private funds will be reduced; the inflated valuations of assets will be slowly deflated; and the returns from your investments will be depleted due to excessive management fees while your investment capital will remain tied up.

Market Implications: Winners, Losers, and What Could Go Right

As an analyst, it’s my responsibility to highlight that risks are building and deserve close attention from investors, but most importantly, it’s my goal to tell you where to invest your money. If you don’t take proactive steps when disasters occur, you’re leaving yourself open to missing a huge investment opportunity. That’s exactly what I am doing with my current investments. This is how I am structuring my portfolio currently.

The "Black List" (The things I'll never invest in)

I will not get into any unlisted or semi-liquid private credit funds. Those fund managers (and we've seen this with Blackstone's BCRED and Blue Owl), as they fight for the valuation of their hard-to-value assets, have the power to freeze your money in place when you try to withdraw.

I'm still concerned with many different types of investment that are extremely high in leverage and require continuous financing. The small-cap sector of the U.S. equity markets fits into this category. In addition to being subject to higher debt servicing costs as interest rates rise, these companies can be severely impacted by deteriorating financial conditions. As such, I have no interest in investing in the Russell 2000 Index. When a business is small, it usually gets crushed the most during an economic downturn and therefore a debt-crushing event.

We understand that there are tensions in the private credit market, and denying this would be biased. What interests us at this stage, however, is understanding how these tensions can be transmitted across different asset classes, whether they remain limited or evolve into something broader.

“Predators” (Who I Believe Are Going To Dominate The Market)

There are always some people that suffer financially from a financial crisis and some people that benefit. I believe that the predators will be successful. I've been purchasing small amounts of shares in large private equity companies (such as Apollo, KKR, and Ares) because the share price of all three has fallen by a significant amount due to public fear. Although their share price has decreased, they have an enormous amount of cash reserves ("dry powder") available to make new investments. The larger private equity companies, like Apollo and KKR, will buy out the remaining assets from collapsing small credit funds at a fraction of the cost.

Private equity giants such as Ares, KKR, and Apollo are expected to make around a 50% gain (see above, source: Bloomberg Opinion). In addition, while retail investors are exiting the market, Wall Street is well aware of who will ultimately emerge victorious in this fight.

The "Fed Put"

I am reading pundits left and right screaming "the end of the world as we know it," and "get ready for a Lehman Brothers 2.0." As I see it, this is an overly pessimistic and fatalistic approach.

A systemic “Lehman-style” event appears unlikely in my view, although localized stress remains a key risk to monitor.

Major U.S. Banks are exposed to private credit through numerous vehicles, including direct lending. However, central bankers and regulators have learned the lessons from 2008 the hard way. Major U.S. Banks now have a much larger cushion against absorbing writedowns of massive amounts of private debt. It will hurt; however, it will be a limited amount.

But what if I'm wrong? What if the panic gets worse and worse, the secondary market selling becomes uncontrolled, and systemic contagion threatens the foundation of Wall Street? Then, we find ourselves staring at the big elephant in the room: the "Fed Put."

Don't make a mistake: the Federal Reserve will not stand idly by while the economy burns in order to teach some unwary private equity fund a lesson. The enormous (or should I say ENORMOUS) mistake that was made in 2008 will NOT be repeated. When there is extreme systemic risk (think 2020 pandemic level), the central bank will inject liquidity, provide emergency loans, or dramatically cut interest rates to slow down the bleeding. I am not writing this because I am 100 percent certain; nor am I writing this because I have had a confidential meeting with a current Fed Chair. I am writing this because the mistake of 2008 still exists today, nearly 15 years after the fact. The Fed, the United States, and the rest of the world cannot afford to experience another Lehman Brothers. Everything will be done to keep the system afloat without too much disruption to the real economy.

That does not mean we can simply ignore everything because the Fed is there. But I believe comparing 2026 to 2008 is way beyond even my wildest imagination.

Do you agree?

data.title: Soaring Bond Yields, Falling Yen, And Big Wage Gains Leave BOJ In A Dither
data.description:
Introduction

Two months ago, I wrote “Hints of U.S. Treasury Intervention To Support The Yen,” where U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent initiated a rate check by the New York Fed to stabilize the yen-dollar exchange rate. Bessent was concerned about rising bond yields in the US, which he tied to rising yields in Japan.

In consultation with his counterparts in Japan, Bessent wanted to reduce the volatility in the markets.

Initially, the move worked. The yen rallied from 159.5 to 152.5, a 4.6% gain.

It also helped Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s Liberal Democratic Party gain a supermajority of 316 out of 465 seats in parliament’s lower house during the snap election two weeks later. This was a big boost from her coalition’s 232 seats before the election.

But the currency improvement was short-lived.

Yen At Risk Of Breaching 160

The recent market moves in response to the conflict in the Middle East have caused the yen to again test the critical 160 exchange rate. This has been the threshold that has triggered intervention in the past.

In Spring 2024, the BOJ, at the behest of the Finance Ministry, bought yen at 160, only to be followed by another round of purchases at 161 two months later. Together, over 15 trillion yen (almost $100 billion) was spent to support the currency.

The Ministry of Finance has indicated that they are monitoring the situation and that the government will take the necessary steps to respond to speculative moves.

Inflation Remains Elevated

Inflation, as measured by Core Core CPI, has been above the 2.0% target for more than two years.

While the February report dropped to 2.5%, its fourth consecutive decline, it has artificially been suppressed by subsidies for electricity and energy. Additionally, the impact of the oil price shock and supply chain disruptions from the Middle East conflict have not yet appeared.

Wage Inflation Persists

On the labor front, the Japanese Trade Union Confederation, known as Rengo, just negotiated a 5.26% wage increase for its workers. This is the third consecutive year with a pay gain exceeding 5% and is the largest wage agreement in 35 years.

Wage gains have been strong due to Japan’s extremely tight labor market. The unemployment rate is 2.7%. Due to Japan’s aging population, they are confronted with a shortage of workers.

Bond Yields At Highest Level Since 1997

Bond yields have been surging in Japan for almost seven years. At the low, during the summer of 2019, the 10-yr JGB was yielding -0.21%.

It is hard for us to imagine now why anyone would buy a bond with a negative yield.

Since then, yields have continuously climbed to the current 2.30%, the highest level in almost 30 years.

Yields have surged for a variety of reasons.

The first is that the largest owner of JGBs has stopped buying. The BOJ was the first central bank to implement Quantitative Easing, as they started in 1999. Through QE, they expanded their balance sheet with large purchases of JGBs. From 72 trillion yen, the bank’s balance sheet ballooned more than tenfold to a peak of 764 trillion yen.

Then, in late summer 2024, BOJ began their policy normalization program and began Quantitative Tightening. Through QT, they stopped buying JGBs and began shrinking their balance sheet.

The government still had financing needs for their fiscal deficit. As such, rates rose to attract buyers.

Since BOJ implemented QT, their share of JGB ownership has dipped from 54% to the current 49%.

The second reason for the rise in yields is that, as mentioned above, inflation has been elevated over the BOJ’s target of 2% for over two years. This puts pressure on yields.

Additionally, expected inflationary effects arising from the Middle East conflict move the yields higher.

A third reason why JGB yields have been rising is due to the illiquidity of JGBs. Since the BOJ has stepped away from buying more JGBs, there has been a void as to who will be the next buyer. This has led to illiquidity in the market.

The Bloomberg JGB Liquidity Index has shown deterioration since the BOJ began QT. At that time, the index was at 3, and it continued to widen, peaking at 9 in January, when the U.S. Treasury did their rate check. It has improved somewhat but is still at a high level of almost 7.

The final reason rates are rising is the yield differentials between Japan and the rest of the developed world. Japan has the highest debt/GDP level of any country in the world. While we are worried about America’s debt levels climbing to 120% of our GDP, Japan’s government debt outstanding exceeds 240% of GDP. Yet, their inflation rates are more similar to ours.

Their interest rate structure, however, is much lower than in the U.S. While the Fed kept its policy rate unchanged at 3.5%-3.75% last week, the BOJ also kept their policy rate unchanged, but it is only 0.75%.

BOJ has been trying to normalize rates, but they are moving very slowly. And the market is demanding more.

The BOJ Dithers

The chart below shows the 10-yr JGB yield compared to the BOJ policy rate. While the BOJ is normalizing rates, they are moving quite slowly. They are always playing catch-up.

The BOJ could have raised rates last week due to the weakness in the yen, the elevated inflation on core CPI and wages, and the fallout from the Middle East conflict, but they did nothing.

BOJ Governor Kazuo Ueda, instead, kept the possibility of a future interest rate hike in April on the table. He was still weighing whether the impacts from the Middle East will be short-lived or not.

This is how the BOJ works; they move glacially. Yet, the bond markets react quickly, and as the chart above shows, with 10-yr JGB yields spiking, they are demanding a policy adjustment from the BOJ.

Financial Impact On BOJ Of Rising Rates

Although it is little discussed, there is a big financial impact on the BOJ of the rise in interest rates.

The first impact is on the market value of the JGBs that the BOJ holds in its portfolio.

The BOJ’s fiscal year ends on March 31st.

Since last year, yields have risen substantially across the board. The following shows the JGB yield curve last year and presently.

The 2-yr JGB yield has risen 46 basis points to 1.29%

The 10-yr JGB yield has risen 82 basis points to 2.30%

The 30-yr JGB yield has risen 104 basis points to 3.56%

Last year, the BOJ reported a large unrealized loss on their bond holdings, as they were purchased when rates were low, and they are significantly higher now. Last year unrealized loss on the bond portfolio was 28 trillion yen.

I estimate that this yield shift will cause the unrealized loss on the JGB portfolio for the BOJ to be 48 trillion yen.

To put this in perspective, the BOJ only has 6 trillion yen in capital.

The BOJ will also experience a realized loss from operations for the first time, as their interest expense will exceed the interest they earn for the fiscal year ending March 31, 2026.

Conclusion

The U.S. Treasury did a rate check in support of the dollar/yen exchange rate in January. The immediate impact was a 4.6% strengthening in the yen.

However, the improvement was short-lived.

Since then, elevated inflation, 5%+ wage gains, and the new Middle East conflict have all put upward pressure on bond yields and the yen.

While the BOJ could have hiked rates in support of the currency and the bond market, they dithered and would only keep open the possibility of a rate hike in April.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Coda Octopus: A Risky Bet On Future Growth From Military Applications
data.description:
Introduction

Coda Octopus Group, Inc. (CODA) caught my attention because of the decent price correction it experienced recently, and now that it has had its Q1 results recently published, I thought it would be a good time to see if it would be a good time to invest. I feel like with small-cap stocks like CODA, there is a lot of risk involved, but from time to time, I like to take some risks and invest in smaller companies, as the growth potential is a lot more enticing. Therefore, I will be starting a position shortly, as I like the product and the outlook and the fact that it gives me more diversification away from the AI craze.

Briefly on the Company

CODA is a small underwater tech company that sells and rents high-end sonar and diving systems that aid the diver to see and work underwater in zero visibility. Most of the money it makes is from its top-of-the-line real-time 3D imaging sonar product, called Echoscope, and the next big driver in the future, DAVD. AR diver helmet system, used in subsea construction as well as many subsea defense applications.

By the Numbers

Starting from the top, revenue came in at $6.7m, up around 29% y/y, which is respectable. Looking at the breakdown in sales in more detail, we can see that the Marine Technology Business, its core sonar, brought it $3.4m of the total revenues and grew 47.4% y/y from $2.3m. MTB represents around 50% of the company’s total revenue. The defense engineering services brought in around $1.8m, up a little over 9% y/y, while its Acoustic Sensors & Materials business grew almost 21% y/y to bring in the rest of the revenues of $1.6m.

Let’s get a little more granular. Within Marine Tech, equipment sales brought in around $2.27m in sales, translating to 31% growth y/y, while rental revenue brought in around $750K, which may not look like a lot, but it’s around 233% growth y/y from $220K last year. Echoscope and DAVD helped the equipment sales’ performance, while the European rental hub drove the rental hub performance.

It is worth noting that Echoscope is said to account for around 80% of Marine Tech revenue, with its sales to Asia growing a whopping 63.4% to $2.6m, so the majority of sales come from Asia.

Let’s take a look at the profitability and efficiency of CODA operations. Overall margins decreased by around 70 bps to 65.1%. This is attributed to a small change in mix and geography, but particularly due to the Defense Engineering Service business performance, as well as the consolidated commission costs increase. Operating margin, on the other hand, increased by around 240 bps due to sales growth exceeding expense growth, which helps increase the company’s cost leverage.

Now digging deeper into the margin profiles of each segment, the “Products” [MTB] saw its gross margins increase from 73.1% to 75.3%, or 220 bps. The Acoustic Sensors and Materials business gross margin was 66.8%, which showed an increase of 510 bps. That is quite an improvement. The Defense Engineering Services gross margin decreased by quite a bit, by 1,480 bps to be exact, and no, that is not a typo. The segment’s efficiency decreased by almost 15%. This drop in efficiency can be attributed to the US funding delays that slowed the awarding of contracts. So, what this essentially means is that its product mix slightly shifted this past quarter. A higher share of work must have come from lower-margin contracts, as opposed to the very lucrative federal ones. I see this as a temporary problem, and I don’t doubt we will see the margins improving once again.

Going over the company’s financial position this past quarter, CODA finished Q1 ’26 with around $30.4m in cash and equivalents against essentially no debt on its books, apart from some operating lease liabilities. The company’s cash position is exceptional. CODA is a small-cap company. Cash alone represents 22% of the company’s valuation. The company is in a very strong position and will no doubt continue to operate and improve further. Additionally, cash from operations more than doubled to $1.64m, and free cash flow improved to $1.39m from $513K. That’s a really good result for a company of this size.

Overall, I would say the company is growing at a very decent and sustainable pace. The results showed good growth across the board, and even the decrease in margins in DES, I see it as a fluke that will sort itself out over the next few quarters. CODA’s balance sheet is very strong, and its cash generation is also noteworthy.

Comments on the Outlook

Let’s dig a little deeper into the company’s premier product offerings to get a better look at what is going to drive CODA’s success in the future. Starting with Echoscope.

Echoscope

As the name implies, Echoscope generates thousands of acoustic beams per ping and renders a live, geo-referenced 3D map, or an acoustic video, like echolocation of whatever is happening deep underwater. It doesn’t need light as it can map even in zero-visibility waters. There are a lot of uses for this product, including port and harbor security, subsea construction, as well as cable/pipeline installation and inspection/repair/maintenance, or IRM for short. What is also very useful for that can save lives is the clearing of old unexploded ordnance or munitions that didn’t detonate properly and may contain live explosives. The product allows to locate these weapons, to identify what they are, and then either removing them or destroying them in a safe manner. This product is very easy to operate and can be mounted on pretty much any sort of ship imaginable. It’s very versatile, which is the big reason for its success.

The management was very positive on the next-gen series of Echoscope, the NanoGen series, which is designed to be much smaller but just as powerful, if not more, to lead the operational performance improvements in the future. This model is also specifically designed to be mounted on autonomous and unmanned subsea vehicles and diver wearables. In the latest opening remarks of the quarter, the management said that they see the UUV market expansion going from around $4.8B to $11B by 2030. How much of the expansion CODA is going to capture here remains to be seen, but these numbers look promising. We won’t have to wait too long to see now, since the management expects to see initial adoption under defense-funded PIPs starting in Q3-Q4 of this year. I’m pretty sure it is going to be a tailwind.

Diver Augmented Vision Display

Or, as it is known as DAVD, it is the company’s next big bet on growth, calling it a disruptive diver technology, which is an end-to-end diver management system that provides a complete topside-plus-diver system with a see-through head-up display that is integrated into the diver’s helmet. This essentially means the diver and the operator up top see the same image. In the image below, you will see an example of what the diver will see in zero-visibility water conditions. This is achievable with the help of Echoscope, overlaying 3D sonar imagery, showing waypoints, models, maps, video, and other sensor data in the diver’s helmet. All these features help with improving situational awareness dramatically. This is particularly important for extremely risky operations, whether military or otherwise. I can see how this could be a popular product among the federal clients.

Currently, the untethered DAVD variant is undergoing Navy safety qualifications and authorization, with the expectation to have this resolved in the next quarter, which will help DAVD’s revenues accelerate and improve margins overall. The management said that there are multiple defense programs already very interested in having DAVD integrated as a core life-support and visualization component, so I am expecting quite good things in the future going forward.

The good thing about DAVD is that it is not dependent solely on the U.S. for revenue generation. It has qualifications in the U.S. and EU, so the market reach is immense, but I’m sure the US will be the leader here once everything comes through.

The application of these products doesn’t rely on governmental defense programs, but I am going to bet that we will see a lot of growth over the next year or so, specifically from these defense programs, not just in the U.S. but globally.

Many countries are on edge right now due to all of the geopolitical risks and escalations of warfare across many different regions, like Iran and Ukraine, which will most likely lead to many governments increasing their military defense spending over the next few years, and that means upgrading naval and underwater infrastructure to be able to protect their respective regions from harm. Recent contracts like the U.S. and foreign navy orders show that defense customers are indeed actively upgrading their capabilities due to more troublesome waters that might still be ahead.

Risks and Closing Comments

There may be further delays in the U.S. defense budgets that delay awards further, creating lumpiness in the operations of the company. This can be seen as a negative because the business is not improving.

DAVD specifically is partially time-dependent on U.S. Navy approvals and procurement cycles. The management still expects everything to go smoothly with the ramp-up, but that can always change in just one quarter if there are further delays.

Further delays may continue to push the company’s margin profile, specifically in the DES, further down, which is obviously a negative.

Concentration of revenues is also a risk. As we found out earlier, around 80% of Marine Tech revenues are from echoscopes, so if there is any slowdown in sales due to competition or a tech disruption, that would materially impact CODA’s operations. Asia, being the main revenue region, is also at risk if the U.S. decides to impose export controls across the globe on materials and products.

Despite these risks, I am very much interested in the company’s future, mainly because I saw very little regarding AI and data centers, which I am already tired of, and I am actively looking to diversify away from them. Therefore, I am going to assign it a buy rating and will look to start a position over the next week, depending on what the week ahead holds. I see quite a bit of potential over the next couple of years. It is a risky bet, but I’d like to make some risky bets from time to time.

data.title: BWX: High Oil Prices Will Translate Into Higher Global Rates (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

The Iran war is not a locally contained issue but one that has ripple effects throughout the world's financial system. While as of now an investor can observe high oil prices and increasing costs at the pump, the secondary effects down the line are very substantial and will affect central bank monetary policy in the next year.

A less visible effect of skyrocketing oil prices has been a reset in global rates, with yields spiking higher as market participants realized the Iran conflict might take longer than expected. In today's article we are going to revisit the SPDR Bloomberg International Treasury Bond ETF (BWX), a global bond ETF we covered two years ago. In that article we described the fund composition and its reaction to spiking rates in 2022, as well as our view on the investment vehicle. In today's article we are going to show readers why a prolonged crisis in the Middle East will likely negatively impact BWX via higher global rates.

What does BWX hold?

Let us start by looking at the fund's stated objective:

The Bloomberg Global Treasury ex-US Capped Index is designed to track the fixed-rate local currency sovereign debt of investment grade countries outside the United States. The Index includes government bonds issued by investment grade countries outside the United States, in local currencies, that have a remaining maturity of one year or more and are rated investment grade.

The ETF therefore holds international government bonds that are rated investment grade. The name does not hedge its currency exposure, thus providing for upside in a weak dollar environment. The main geographies currently present in the fund are as follows:

  • Japan: 23.3%
  • UK: 5%
  • France: 4.9%
  • China: 4.7%
  • Canada: 4.6%
  • Germany: 4.6%
  • Spain: 4.5%
  • Italy: 4.5%
  • South Korea: 4.4%
  • Australia: 4%
  • Belgium: 3.7%
  • Netherlands: 2.9%

The ETF has a very wide geographic reach and is diversified. Some analytics for the name are:

  • Number of holdings: 1,671
  • Option-adjusted duration: 7.4 years
  • Portfolio yield to maturity: 3.32%
  • Portfolio OAS: 11.24 bps
  • SEC yield: 2.82%
  • AUM: $1.5 B
  • Expense ratio: 0.35%

Given its investment-grade government build, the fund has very low yields, commensurate with local central bank rate policies. The main driver for gains for the ETF is represented by global rates, and we can see the severe negative impact on the fund in 2022 when yields spiked:

When global yields move higher, the fund goes down in price given its duration of 7.4 years. The current yield is fairly small at 2.26%; thus, duration and global rates are the main profit and loss drivers here. Let us look at how global rates are doing.

Higher oil prices are bringing back the specter of inflation

While investors initially thought the conflict would be a short one, the optics have changed dramatically as of late:

Three weeks later, the prospect of a drawn-out war is causing mounting economic problems. Oil prices have soared above $100 a barrel, European gas prices have doubled, volatility stalks financial markets, and consumers worldwide are bracing for a surge in living costs. Central banks, including the US Federal Reserve, Bank of England and European Central Bank, warn the war could have a material impact on inflation and dent global growth.

Financial markets are not waiting for the end results but have reacted, moving government yields higher:

In the above charts, which present the UK 10-year yields and Japan 10-year rates, we can clearly see the spike higher as the Iran conflict started. While the front end of a yield curve embeds central bank monetary policy, the long end reacts to a multitude of factors, including inflation expectations. When the market believes inflation might start going up again, it moves rates higher in order to be compensated for it.

Although the long end of the curve has already moved for most of the held countries, the front end is following soon, with central banks set to now hike rates:

The quantum of hikes and the net impact on the long end will be determined by the ever-evolving situation in Iran and the ultimate result of the conflict. A prolonged war that results in significant infrastructure destruction and thus forces less volume even after the conflict ends is inflationary in nature. The more the conflict drags on and destroys oil infrastructure, the higher the long end of government curves. The picture is very clear for all central banks:

Top central banks have said they stand ready to tackle any surge in inflation with tighter policy as the latest escalation in the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran puts the Middle East's vital energy infrastructure in the line of fire, pushing fuel prices higher. In a rare coincidence of the monetary policy diary, central banks of the United States, Japan, Britain, Canada and the euro zone – effectively the Group of Seven (G7) nations — convened this week, as have counterparts from several emerging economies.

The impact of higher long rates on BWX was immediate:

The fund has had a -3% drawdown, basically accounting for a year of dividends. Do expect the name to move lower if the resolution to the conflict is not fast and swift.

Risks are skewed to the downside

At the current juncture we are witnessing a geopolitical situation without a clear resolution, where the goals of the Iran conflict are not readily transparent. There are constant updates on the war from both sides, with conflicting messages. The fear here is a long conflict that will keep oil prices high, which in turn will create a massive inflationary wave:

Brent oil is up an astounding +69% this year, creating the premise for significant price pressures across a large segment of goods:

The above chart from Goldman does a nice job of quantifying the impact on headline inflation under a base case and adverse scenarios. In the Euro Area, for example, we can see inflation moving up by 3% in the most adverse scenario.

As an investor, you want to buy assets that offer good rewards for the risk taken and sell those that have more downside than upside. BWX offers a mere 2.8% SEC yield and the potential for another leg down in price if the Iran conflict does not end swiftly. We are a 'Sell' for the name here.

Conclusion

BWX is a fixed-income exchange-traded fund. The name is an aggregator for global government bonds that hold an investment-grade rating. The fund is negatively impacted by higher global rates and has seen its price decrease on the back of the Iran war. The longer the conflict lasts, the higher the global inflationary pressures, thus putting pressure on rates and bond prices. With risks skewed to the downside here and a low 2.8% SEC yield, we are a 'Sell' for BWX in the current macro.

data.title: The Shape Of Oil/Energy Shipping: Looking At Frontline
data.description:
It's fair to say that shipping, especially the transport and shipping of oil/energy, is one of the more volatile businesses that there is to be invested in and to look at. Over the course of the start of the Iran war, I've slowly researched more and become a bit more comfortable with potentially investing in the sector. I haven't done so yet, but I may do so in the future.

Part of the reason is that energy shipping is fairly "close to home" for me. Many of the companies, even the one I am covering today, are actually owned by businesses or businessmen with ties or origins in Scandinavia. Many of them are even listed at "home" on our stock markets. This one is an example of such a company.

Frontline plc (FRO) has both an NYSE ticker, FRO, and an Oslo ticker in the NOK FX, OSE: FRO. This means that it's extremely appealing on a basis of FX diversification for those available for investing here, while also allowing U.S. investors to get exposure to what essentially amounts to a comparatively small business in energy.

I say small in terms of net income and revenue compared to other companies listed on the stock market, not small in terms of its size.

If you want to invest in these companies, you need to leave traditional notions of value investing behind and focus on the shorter term and understanding industries/sectors. That's the biggest challenge I have with this entire sector. You need to watch commodity cycle trends, and you need to be comfortable forecasting a lot more and reacting/estimating when those forecasts aren't materialized. You also need to have a long-term holding period. Because, as we've seen in the past, shipping can stay down for years - sometimes a decade. That does not mean that the assets you own shares in are bad or worth selling.

If you look at the valuation of Frontline, you'll find very little that makes sense to you unless you understand the sector.

In this article, I will attempt to provide you with some basic understanding and show you how I think about the sector and about Frontline.

Frontline - Upside From Shipping in This Environment

Frontline is a PLC, not an ASA. This means that the company is not incorporated in Norway but in a common law jurisdiction - in this case, Cyprus - even if Norwegian law, due to its financing, dictates some of its major moves (and with a management company incorporated in Bermuda). It has a market cap (Norway) of nearly 70B NOK for the ticker; for the U.S., it has assets of about $5.8B (mostly tied up in vessels) and revenues per year of about $2B. These give them a net income between $380M and $650M in good years, which can go negative in bad years, as you see below.

Frontline's origins is originally a Swedish company, but about 30 years ago it was bought up by (meaning the largest shareholder), Billionaire John Fredriksen. The company has gone and absorbed multiple shipping companies, such as London and Overseas almost directly after his takeover, ICB, and Golden Ocean.

This has made Frontline one of the largest tanker companies - a position it still holds in 2026.

Frontline is a very solidly capitalized company. By this, I mean that when I analyze the balance sheet, I find very little material or objective weakness. With over $700M in cash and equivalents and no significant debt maturing for the next 4 years, the company is in no immediate worries no matter what happens.

The last few years have been active for Frontline. The company has been both selling and buying new assets/renovations. In 2025 (4Q report), the company sold 8 VLCCs for over $800M, which, net of debt, generated almost $480M in cash proceeds - taking advantage of a currently pressured market while at the same time improving its own appeal by buying ECO VLCC newbuildings at close to $1.25B.

The company's fleet, as of today, consists of 41 VLCCs, 21 Suezmaxes, and 18 LR2s. The company is in the process of fitting scrubbers and ESG on these, where this is not done.

The company has very attractive breakeven rates. This "breakeven" rate represents what the company has to earn (minimum) in freight to cover expenses. The company's breakeven rates for its VLCCs are $25,000 per day, and this is the highest. To give you a comparison, recent conflicts have inflated Atlantic freight to over $60,000 per day,-and I don't see this going down anytime soon.

In a good operating environment, which we technically have right now, FRO does very well for itself. It's a cash-generative business, as evidenced by over $660M in FCF in the last reported fiscal year. However, it's also incredibly volatile in this (and other metrics), as we can easily see on a historical basis.

It's fair to say that Frontline plc goes up and down with the trends in the industry. Now, FCF might not be the best metric to gauge this company, but given the recent spike, I at least wanted to show this to you. The reason why FCF is a sub-par metric is due to the extreme capital intensity of the business. Frontline can be doing very well, and FCF may still look poor. Instead, we need to turn our eyes to the commodities market.

It's fair to say that demand for shipping is on the rise. It's further fair to say that the demand for shipping will continue to rise. Oil transportation companies with a modern, EU-attractive fleet are in a prime position to take advantage of this market. The market is underserved by newbuilds, which is why the company is asset recycling, with asset prices firmly going up. It will be some time before things go "down" here.

The geopolitical volatility that seems to be characterizing the current macro, not only in Iran but elsewhere as well, is fertile ground for Frontline PLC to generate profits. Global crude oil in transit continues to be higher than it has been for a long time, and imports, both globally but above all in the Far East/India, continue to go up. Exports, before the war, were going up or at high levels from OPEC - now that Iran is blocking Hormuz with no immediate end in sight, these tailwinds have intensified.

Looking at shipbuilder order books, most of the orders until 2029 are concerning Containers, Bulkers and LNG - crude tankers aren't on the rise until 2029 and beyond. Provided the market remains supportive, the demand for VLCC (Very Large Crude Carriers), is expected to not only be maintained, but to rise further.

Overall, I like what I see. I may be somewhat of a newcomer investor in the tanker/shipping market, but I know how to analyze good fundamentals. The company has good fundamentals. You have to realize, of course, that Frontline doesn't care for your "dividend tradition." It pays out what it can, and it doesn't care how the dividend level moves. In 2022 the company dividend was 1.52 NOK. In 2024, it was 21 NOK. For 2026, it's now expected to be 54.56 NOK. The company decides, strictly based on profit, how much it pays out. Given the capital intensity, this is exactly what they should be doing.

With its current fleet size operating 365, the company has 30,000+ days of earnings per year, and if you look at what the daily cost of a tanker is today, you can quickly add that up to a lot of cash generation. What's more, FRO is a net beneficiary of sanctions and tariffs.

Why?

Because they lower the utilization of assets, bringing forth Frontline's compliant assets.

The so-called compliant crude is the oil that's up. Sanctioned crude has been flat for a long time, with Iran's the only one showing even slight growth (before the war, obviously).

So, Frontline is volatile - yes. But Frontline is also probably one of the most appealing companies in this entire sector, if you're so inclined to invest. They combine what I consider to be extremely safe, well-managed operations with what can only be described as low levels of debt (less than 50% of equity). And while FCF and EPS may be volatile, there's one metric that has stayed positive through every market - OCF. For over 10 years, FRO has managed positive operational cash flow, which has been rising more and more as the company's assets and balance sheet has improved.

Let me, therefore, show you how I would value the company at this time.

Valuing Frontline PLC - A Tricky Venture, but Far From Impossible

Shipping companies are valued using a mix of method with a high degree of certainty (or at least, higher), with methods that are more speculative. There's unfortunately no helping this, no matter how "certain" some analysts want to seem in their forecasts.

The simpler method with high certainty is NAV. We look at net asset value because the ships are the assets (minus debt), and these make up what the company "is." The only uncertainty in this method is value appreciation for the vessels, which goes to market value trends, and asset age. Both of these factors are more manageable (infinitely so) than the other approach.

Because the other approach is usually an income-based method. Unfortunately, as we've established, forecasting a company's cash flow or earnings on charter rates is fairly likened to using a crystal ball. Because of the volatility in these sectors, I've taken an approach that more heavily leans towards the former, because this is the method we can actually control. The most recent numbers here suggest assets of $5.8B, which, net of debt, come to about $2.5B in equity (and adding cash back), which comes to a net asset value per share of about $12.4/share as of the company's full-year filings. Based on a current share price of ~$33/share, it operates currently significantly above its NAV, but it was less than a year ago, that it operated close to NAV, at below $14.5/share.

This spike reflects the estimated surge in earnings for 2026.

This is basically the market "guessing" what will happen during the year. Current price targets, or PTs, reflect this. The PT about 1.5 years ago was less than $27.5/share. Now the average, from 4 analysts, is $41.25/share with a high-end target of $46/share. This reflects the high cyclicality. Going by market valuations of NAV (S&P Global), the company has gone between lows of 0.36x and highs of close to 2x, currently at 1.17x.

The trick here is finding a good middle ground between the currently significant surging targets due to the fundamentals in the sector, and the more conservative long-term trends that we can see for between 2010 and 2020. I would argue that, based on the company's fleet renewals and upgrades, Frontline is in a prime position even going forward. I have little issue allowing a bit of a premium for the company.

Because of achieving high TCE earnings in Q4 ($74,200 per day for the VLCC), with a very low fleet age (100% eco-friendly, less than 8 years average age), a very limited market, a very conservative capital allocation policy, and solid (as I see it) management, I raise my expectations for the Q1 '26 earnings. I believe carrier rates are likely to be sustained. The company is trading at a significant premium in P/E to most of its peers - over 18x based on 2025, compared to 13x-15x average, but the combination of continued high spot rates with a very attractive fleet makes a strong case for this being valid.

Put another way, I don't believe 2026 will be a bad year for earnings. And if it's not bad, it's likely to be good - very good. While I will discount the current estimate of $6/share by at least 20%, that still puts us at a 2026E P/E of 8.25x - undervalued at $33/share. Even if we normalize the coming 3 years at 40-50% lower than 2026E, which I believe to be fair, that brings us to a 3-year average of $3.33/share, or, based on $33/share, a P/E of about 10x.

That is technically undervalued.

Even if I further discount the PT and assumptions, I get to a 20% annualized rate of return (with the estimated 9-11% dividend yield based on $3.33 EPS) until 2028, which justifies a PT of $34.5/share.

That makes the company, as it stands today, a Buy - if only a slim one.

Thesis

Frontline PLC is a net beneficiary of the current market trends. It has one of the most modern tanker fleets in the world, and is also one of the largest companies in its field, in the entire world. While I typically am careful about covering this, and I do view this company as a speculative investment, I also view it as attractive here.

Based on the company's estimate accuracy, fundamentals, and yield, I would put a $34.5/share price target. I would, based on the current company share price and estimates, give the company a Buy rating, but would also make it clear that this is a speculative rating.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (in italics).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a price target of $34.5/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a Buy rating as of March 2026.

data.title: SkyWater Technology Shows Strong Revenue Gains But Profitability Remains Weak
data.description:
My view on SkyWater Technology (SKYT) has shifted since I last rated it. Back then, I called it a Hold. I was worried about how much the company needed to execute just right and I thought the big growth hopes were mostly priced in already. At that time, I figured 2026 would be the important test. The stock had already tripled off the April lows, with about over 80% growth over the year. I said, “today’s price already reflects a lot of that middle-to-bullish scenario. So there’s real downside risk if the execution isn’t almost perfect… I’d want to see at least a couple more quarters of good, steady results before feeling more confident about the stock”.

A few things have changed since then. The stock backed up some of the more optimistic stories, hitting record consolidated revenue for FY2025, $442.1 million, up 29% from the year before. Also, SkyWater actually pulled off some things that used to be big question marks. The Fab 25 integration, which looked risky at first, ended up better than expected in both finances and operations. It brought in $175.6 million in revenue in its first full year for SkyWater. That’s not just a nice bump; it’s a shift in scale. Now SkyWater is the biggest US pure-play foundry at the basic chip nodes, with its share of the 200mm foundry market estimated to have increased from roughly 4% to about 17%.

Profitability Pressure

Still, my earlier caution sticks when it comes to profits and how reliable they’ll be. Non-GAAP gross margin for 2025 was 20.7%. Adjusted EBITDA for the full year came in at $53.2 million, or a 12% margin, up from 10% last year. But the actual bottom line? Still pretty thin: non-GAAP net income was just $0.7 million, about $0.01 per diluted share. On the official (GAAP) numbers, profits were skewed by one-time gains from the Fab 25 deal. What really stands out: revenues from the original SkyWater business went down 22% during this period. That’s a big drop hiding under all the new revenue from Fab 25 and it’s not something you can just ignore. The old part of the business is shrinking, and while quantum and advanced packaging are growing, they’re still smaller and haven’t proven they can deliver strong enough margins to pull the whole company out of the low-to-mid teens zone.

Valuation and Balance Sheet

Valuation’s still kind of a hang-up for going all-in on a Buy here. Market cap sits at about $1.3 billion and if you look at the forward revenue estimate, around $600 million based on company guidance, the price-to-sales ratio comes out to about 2.2. Forward EV/EBITDA is up at about 23.5, which is a lot higher than what some peers usually see and forward P/E is still in the red because they keep posting net losses. Peer sales multiples aren’t totally out of whack for a US-based foundry with high-growth stuff going on, but the fact that they haven’t nailed down consistent profits and have ongoing working capital headaches makes it hard to say the stock is really cheap. Cash flow from operations for the whole year was negative, at approximately $(24.1) million. The company also carries a meaningful debt load and relies on financing facilities, so the balance sheet doesn’t give them much cushion if they mess up execution or if government and quantum spending slows down. If that happens, they’ll probably have to raise more money and could end up diluting existing shareholders.

Growth Drivers

SkyWater’s “domestic and trusted” angle has definitely helped them win business in quantum, government and aerospace. Management is now saying they see at least $600 million in 2026 revenue, mostly off the back of momentum at Fab 25 and a steady ramp in quantum deals, ATS quantum-related revenue jumped over 30% in 2025. The advanced packaging ramp beat Q4 expectations for both ATS and Tools revenue. Still, the company isn’t turning all that gross profit dollar growth into real free cash flow yet. High capex and some trouble folding everything together are dragging things down.

Operational Risks

A lot could still go wrong here, so it’s hard to get too excited. Even management says timing on government contracts is unpredictable, and scaling up Fab 25 might run into cost overruns. Inflation showed up in a real way in Q4, tooling installs in Florida cost $9.3 million more than expected. That hit the gross margin for the quarter and just drove home how messy operations can get, and you can’t really see all the bumps ahead or hedge them away. If there’s a big slowdown, or if they lose or don’t renew some key quantum or government contracts, sales multiples could drop, and the company would have to deal with already tight leverage and cash.

Investors are still really optimistic right now, with headlines focused on strong results and momentum in quantum, along with Fab 25 performing better than earlier expectations. But after the stock more than quadrupled since April, it’s hard to see the multiple getting any higher unless future upgrades are backed by steady, real profit. Management says they’re aiming for $600 million or more in revenue by 2026, and maybe that’s doable. And with the company filing to potentially raise $350 million through mixed securities in 2025, it looks like they’re leaving the door open to getting more cash if things get tight or if another big scaling opportunity comes up.

IonQ Deal

The $1.8 billion IonQ deal, cash and stock has become a key factor in how investors are valuing the stock, with some treating it partly as a merger-driven situation rather than purely a standalone growth story. But the deal isn’t done. It still needs regulatory and shareholder approval. The company even said, “the inability to consummate the acquisition of SkyWater by IonQ… could cause the Company’s actual results… to be materially different,” which just means if the deal blows up or drags out, the stock could get real volatile.

Hold Rating

I’m sticking with a Hold on SkyWater Technology. Even though they’ve made progress operationally and the revenue quality looks better since Fab 25, the risk/reward at this price doesn’t make sense with the margin swings, ongoing cash burn and the usual headaches from integrating and dealing with market cycles. They have to actually show they can get to 20%-plus non-GAAP gross margins, turn in positive EPS each quarter and really flip free cash flow so it covers all their growth and deal-making. That’s what it’ll take for me to bump my rating.

Right now, both the story and the numbers need to be checked out independently. The market’s still treating this like a growth play, but if even one part of the bullish case cracks, a lot of people could get caught wrong-footed. If you’re being careful, it probably makes sense to wait unless SkyWater puts together several strong quarters in a row, with good cash from operations and margins above 20% non-GAAP, along with steady revenue beats. Until then, I just don’t see enough upside for the risk to make this a Buy.

data.title: The Trade Desk: This Is Getting Ridiculous
data.description:
Late last year, I triple upgraded The Trade Desk, Inc. (TTD) from a sell to a strong buy rating. I believe there was a generational buying opportunity since the long term growth story remained intact and management demonstrated confidence through aggressive share buybacks. As shown in the chart below, The Trade Desk has slid another ~40% since then and so if the fundamentals and outlook have remained unchanged, we may be looking at the bargain of the century. Today, we'll analyze their most recent results and look at some recent news to see whether this is in fact the case right now.

Below, it is shown that the company is indeed feeling the effects of geopolitical and macro headwinds. Top line growth continues to slow while guidance for 2026 Q1 is also soft. While an uncertain backdrop has created significant near term risks, the long term remains bright in my view. Much of the international market remains untapped for The Trade Desk and their potential OpenAI deal signals that their business remains very relevant in the age of AI. Meanwhile, the valuation has continued to slide and so at current levels I view the risk/reward as asymmetrically favorable for the bulls. Therefore, I'm reiterating my strong buy rating on the stock.

Global Expansion Potential

Before we talk about the numbers specific to their 2025 Q4, it is important to look at the big picture opportunity moving forward. The above map is a slide from their investor presentation that does a good job at summing up the growth potential for The Trade Desk.
